data.prose:
# Chapter Two

# Charlotte and the Echo

Minutes before the chandeliers of the Grand Atrium sagged in their wattage and a relay clicked behind the cloakroom wall four hundred and twelve feet below the surface, three nautical miles east of that quiet drop the USS Charlotte ran easy on the upslope, and Commander Leonard Beckwith was alone in his stateroom under a red lamp, signing the daily report.

He dated the line — 14 SEP 79, 2318Z — set down the pencil, and squared the loose pages with the heel of his hand. The stateroom was the small box that came with the office: a narrow bunk, a fold-down desk, a single brass-framed photograph of his wife at a beach he no longer remembered the name of, and a private drawer that the manual permitted him and the manual did not enter. He pulled the drawer open one inch on its felted runner.

Inside lay an envelope. He had addressed it in pencil on Sunday and had not finished writing it; the unfinished page was folded inside, in his own hand, with three crossed-out openings and one that he had let stand. He set his palm flat over the envelope for the span of one breath, the way a man tested a stove without intending to use it, and closed the drawer.

He pulled on his cap and stepped through into the passage.

The Charlotte at this hour was the working sound of a boat at quiet speed: ventilation, the breath of the dehumidifier, the soft chuckle of a coffee urn three frames aft, men's rubber soles laying down their own muted percussion on the deck plates. The red lamps in the overhead made every uniform the same color and every face a degree younger than it was. Beckwith took the passage forward by habit, ducked the low frame at the watertight door, and came through into the forward torpedo room.

The space was clean the way only a torpedo room got clean. Four loaded tubes; the spare pair on their cradle in oiled canvas; the deck so recently swabbed that the chief on watch had laid down a pair of cardboard tracks for him to walk over. Beckwith took them. The chief saluted, made the small jerk of head and shoulder that on Charlotte stood in for a salute, and did not speak. A first-class torpedoman at the after end of the rack lifted his chipped enamel mug off the lip of the loading carriage before Beckwith could see he had left it there, set it on the deck behind his boot, and resumed wiping a bronze fitting with a square of cotton flannel.

"Evening, Chief."

"Evening, Captain."

"How's the spare?"

"Clean and lazy, sir."

"Keep it lazy."

"Aye, sir."

He went back through the watertight frame, gave the dehumidifier its half-second of acknowledgment, and came aft along the passage to the wardroom. Ennis was already at the table with the watch packet open across the green felt, two grease pencils and a coffee at his elbow and his face in the small mid-evening quiet of a man who had reached the second column without being interrupted.

"Captain."

"Carry on, Number One."

Ennis carried on. Beckwith pulled out the chair opposite and turned the top sheet around. The packet was the Hatteras book: a typed list of notices to mariners with the day's amendments inked in by yeoman's hand, a copied weather sheet from the surface forecaster, the exercise-boundary diagram for the eastern Atlantic operating areas with the night's traffic sketched on it in soft pencil, and beneath those, a copied facility-operations notice from the Naval Facility at Hatteras that the yeoman had stapled in because it had come over the wire and the manual told him to staple it.

The notice was a list. Beckwith read it the way he read lists. Surface vessel transits: a Norwegian motor tanker, a Coast Guard buoy tender on its monthly round, a research catamaran out of Beaufort, the cable ship Long Lines making for Bermuda. Submarine exercise boxes in their familiar lettered shapes. Weather: building swell, southwest at twelve to fifteen, sea state three.

Then, lower on the sheet, under a heading the yeoman had underlined twice in his careful hand: COMMERCIAL UNDERSEA INSTALLATION — AURELIA — 34°45′N 75°36′W — DEPTH 412 FT — STANDOFF 500 YDS — OPERATIONS BEGIN THIS DATE. Beneath that line, in the small typed block of facility traffic the surface yeoman had copied down because it was on the same paper: TRITON STAGING; DAPHNE, NEREID CRADLE; PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE — T. REYES — 2200.

Beckwith's eye passed over it and went on, the way it had passed over the buoy tender and the Norwegian tanker. He took his grease pencil and made the small private mark he made on every facility traffic he read: a single dash, off to the left of the line, that meant *seen*.

"Long Lines is south of us at twenty-three," Ennis said. "She'll be off the plot by midnight."

"Good."

"The hotel goes in tonight."

"I read it."

"They'll have a lift cycle running every half hour out of the surface platform for the next several days."

"We'll stay outside the standoff."

"Yes, sir."

The wardroom intercom panel above the table gave the small dry click of a sound-powered phone keying. Cole's voice came over it formal, careful, very young.

"Bridge, Sonar. Bridge, Sonar."

Beckwith took up the handset before Ennis could.

"Bridge."

"Bridge, Sonar. New contact, designate Sierra Three, faint, bearing two-six-eight true. Behavior not consistent with declared exercise traffic in the area. Continuing to evaluate, sir."

"Bearing rate?"

"Slow right drift, sir. Maybe one mil a minute. Stand by."

"Hold the contact, Cole. I'm coming forward."

"Hold the contact, aye."

Beckwith set the handset on its cradle. Ennis was already capping the grease pencil and folding the watch packet to the chart sheet.

"Light enough to be a fishing screw," Ennis said.

"It would have to be a quiet fishing screw."

"Yes, sir."

Beckwith left his coffee where it stood. He went forward.

---

The control room received him without ceremony, the watch turning a quarter into his shape as he came through the door and then turning back to the boards. Red light made the gauges look hand-painted. The damage-control board on the forward bulkhead carried its small tags in their familiar tidy lines, every compartment marked dry and trimmed, the lozenge for Bulkhead Four sitting where it always sat — green, normal, nothing to report. Beckwith took in the board the way he took in a clock: a glance, an interior tick, and on.

The plot was a glass-topped table at the center of the deck with a laminated chart of the Hatteras shelf beneath it and three small wooden markers — one for Charlotte, one for the contact, one floating loose at the table's edge for whatever Cole heard next.

"Sonar, Bridge. Bearing."

"Bridge, Sonar. Two-six-nine. Holding the right drift."

"Any blade rate?"

"Negative blade rate, sir. Broadband only. Quiet."

Beckwith took up his place at the plot beside Ennis. The contact mark lay below them and to the west — Charlotte on the upslope at three hundred and forty feet, the contact slant-range somewhere below four hundred, on a bearing that did not match any declared traffic on the eastern operating area sheets.

"Cole. Run the bearing against the exercise schedule."

"Sir, I ran it. No declared traffic on two-six-nine. Closest declared is bravo box, three-one-zero."

"Acknowledged."

He let the bearing drift another minute. Ennis moved the loose marker a quarter inch on the laminate; the marker squeaked. Charlotte's hull breathed around them — a faint settling somewhere aft as a tank trimmed itself; the air handlers; men keeping their voices down.

"Bridge, Sonar. Contact has shifted bearing. Now two-six-five. Rate increased."

"He's turning."

"Yes, sir," Cole said.

"Or somebody is."

Ennis time-marked the plot with the side of his grease pencil. "Solution?"

"Wait for it."

They waited. The deck creaked once underfoot. Somewhere on the boat a hatch closed with the soft contained sound of a hatch closed by a man who knew the captain was at the plot.

"Bridge, Sonar. Contact has changed depth. Going deeper. I have a thermal layer in his return, sir."

"Cole. Plain language."

"Bridge, Sonar — contact is using the layer. He's putting it between us."

Beckwith laid the side of his hand on the laminate beside the contact's mark and left it there.

"Make your speed five knots. Rig for ultra-quiet."

The order went down the boat in the quiet way orders went down the boat at this hour — a phone-talker speaking it into the circuit, the circuit speaking it forward, fans dropping out of their hum compartment by compartment until the boat sounded the way it sounded only on the nights it sounded that way.

"Bridge, Sonar." Cole's voice had flattened — not louder, just flatter, the practiced flattening of a young man who would rather not be heard sounding as young as he was. "Bridge, Sonar — transient. Mechanical transient from Sierra Three, bearing now two-six-three. Sounds like a tube door, sir. Stand by."

"Time-mark," Beckwith said.

"Time-marked," Ennis said.

"Bridge, Sonar — launch transient. Launch transient from Sierra Three, bearing two-six-three. High-speed screws in the water. Bearing closing."

Ennis's pencil moved. "Time-marked. Twenty-three forty-eight fifteen Zulu."

Beckwith faced the plot. "All ahead full. Left full rudder. Make your depth one-five-zero. Sound the collision alarm."

The collision alarm went, a single hard low note in the overhead that had been designed in 1962 to be heard through a closed pillow. The deck angled under him. Beckwith took the rail at the plot and held it.

"Bridge, Sonar — torpedo in the water, bearing constant. Bearing constant, sir."

"Acknowledged."

"Bearing constant —"

"Acknowledged, Cole."

A Russian voice came out of the overhead intercept then, broken by depth and by the seawater between them — three syllables, perhaps four, in the clipped descending cadence of a command — then the speaker hissed white and was empty.

"Brace," Beckwith said.

He set both palms flat to the plot table and waited for what was coming.

---

It came in his teeth before it came in the lights.

The concussion was less like a strike than like a slap delivered to the whole length of the boat at once: a soundless white shove through bone, hull, deck plates, and air, then the noise catching up — a hard ringing groan through frame and stringer, the small dry crack of breakers somewhere aft, the slamming bark of the lighting panel as the standard lamps cut out and the emergency reds came up. A man at the helm went forward against his belt. Another, behind Beckwith, did not have a belt and went into the chart table. The boat heeled to starboard and steadied and then, slower, took on a faint and unfamiliar bow-down trim.

"Damage report."

The phone-talker had already keyed his circuit. His voice cracked on the first word and he recovered it on the second.

"Forward — forward of Bulkhead Four — flood alarm. Compartment three. Flood alarm at Bulkhead Four."

"Repeat."

"Flooding forward of Bulkhead Four. Compartment three taking water, sir."

On the damage-control board, the lozenge that had been green a minute earlier blinked over to its angry color and stayed there. Two more tags beside it lit up amber as the men forward read their own gauges.

"Dog the door at Four. Cross-connect the trim pumps. I want a pressure on that bulkhead."

The phone-talker repeated the orders into the circuit and the circuit took them forward. Beckwith heard the watertight door at Four go in the way a watertight door went when it was dogged in earnest — a quick triple thud of the handwheel locking down. Somewhere behind it, a pump came on; a second pump answered.

"Bulkhead Four, dogged. Pumps cross-connected. Pressure holding."

"Hold the pressure."

"Holding the pressure, sir."

"Casualty in the head compartment, Captain." Another circuit, another voice. "Lacerations. He's on the wheel."

"Tell him to stay on the wheel."

"Aye, sir."

The lights stayed red.

Beckwith took two breaths. He counted them because they were the only thing he had not yet ordered. The boat's bow-down trim steadied at three degrees. The pumps held their note. Somewhere far forward and below them, water was now where water had not been five minutes before, and was held in the compartment they had given it, and could be lived with if it stayed where it was.

"Sonar, Bridge."

"Bridge, Sonar." Cole's voice had not flattened any further; there was no further to go. "I have Sierra Three bearing two-six-one, slow left drift. He's coming around."

"For another shot."

"Cannot confirm, sir. He's coming around."

"Acknowledged."

---

He looked once at Ennis. Ennis was already at the fire-control table; he kept his eyes on his sheet.

"Solution?"

"Working, sir."

"Work it."

The fire-control men worked it. The plot table received their numbers and gave them back the geometry of a solution that had been, until the launch transient, a tactical exercise on paper and was now the only sentence the boat had left to write. Beckwith set the flat of his right hand on the laminate beside the contact mark, palm down, and held it there for perhaps three seconds.

He spoke for the room and for the log.

"Confirmed sequence. Sierra Three launched, time twenty-three forty-eight fifteen. Charlotte struck, time twenty-three fifty oh-three. Flooding contained at Bulkhead Four. Sierra Three observed turning. Engagement, defensive."

"Engagement, defensive," Ennis repeated.

"Tube one. Match bearings and shoot."

"Match bearings and shoot, tube one, aye."

The torpedoman's voice came back over the circuit, formal, calm, with the half-beat pause before each phrase that men used when they were reading from a checklist they had read a thousand times and now needed to read again.

"Match bearings, aye. Solution set. Tube one ready in all respects."

"Shoot."

"Tube one fired electrically."

The boat did not jar — the weapon left clean, the way a weapon left a boat of her class — and the small jolt in the deck was less than the jolt of a watertight door swinging home. Cole picked up the run almost at once.

"Bridge, Sonar — own ship's weapon, bearing two-six-one. Running normal. Wire is good."

"Acknowledged."

The fire-control men talked the wire down. Ennis time-marked each crossing on the plot. The forward torpedoman's voice came back at intervals on the circuit, level, unhurried, each report half a beat slower than the one before it as if the man on the other end of it were tightening his jaw between transmissions.

It took ninety-two seconds. They were not loud seconds. The sonar hum through the bulkhead set the room's clock more than the boat did. At eighty-three seconds, Cole said, "Bridge, Sonar — high-speed screws on Sierra Three. He's making a run." At eighty-nine, "Bridge, Sonar — own ship's weapon, terminal." At ninety-two —

The hit came over the headphones as something the boat herself could feel a quarter-second later: a great slow underwater collapse, not the bright crack of a surface explosion but the long groan of a pressure hull giving up its argument, layered over by the smaller secondary sounds of bulkheads working against themselves and air finding its way out where it should not be. The boat heard it in her own steel.

Nobody in the control room moved.

Ennis closed his eyes for the span of one breath and opened them again.

"Bridge, Sonar — Sierra Three breaking up. I have — I have multiple returns, sir. Hard returns and soft. He's coming apart."

"Acknowledged."

"Bridge, Sonar — secondary sounds. Bulkheads. Air."

"Acknowledged, Cole."

The torpedoman on the circuit said, with the empty politeness of a man reading from a card he had been issued in school, "Tube one, hit confirmed, sir." He gave no further word. The circuit went back to its breathing.

Beckwith lifted his hand from the laminate. His palm had taken the chart's faint grid in red ink. He set it back on the table at the edge, palm down, and let the room be quiet around him for a moment longer than was strictly operational.

The boat smelled of burnt insulation now, and of the metal taste a man got in his teeth when his teeth had been rattled. Under it, very faintly, ran the smell of damp cotton from the torpedoman's flannel, and the deeper, older smell of pump oil. Beckwith took one breath of all of it. He gave the room the smallest nod he had, which was the only gesture he allowed himself before the work began again.

"Damage control."

"Bulkhead Four, holding, sir. Pressure steady. Pumps holding."

"Casualty?"

"Stable. He's off the wheel. Doc's on him."

"Sonar."

"Bridge, Sonar — Sierra Three is gone as a contact. I have breakup. Multiple bearings."

"Hold the breakup, Cole. I want every return."

"Hold the breakup, aye."

---

Ennis brought the watch packet out of his jacket pocket without being asked and laid it open on the chart table beside the plot. The Hatteras sheet came up. The two of them stood over it.

"Set," Ennis said quietly, "is one hundred ten, true, at three quarters of a knot. The slope falls away at fifteen degrees south-southwest."

"Down to the four-hundred-foot terrace."

"Yes, sir."

Beckwith took the grease pencil. He marked Sierra Three's last known position with a small open circle. He drew, from the circle, the slow arc of the current set; then, broader, a cone of uncertainty along the down-slope vector, opening with depth. The cone widened on the laminate, and then it widened further, and Ennis added, without speaking, the half-knot allowance for the variable wind-driven set that the surface forecast had given them.

The cone, drawn, lay across a small chart notation: a square with the standoff ring around it, and the typed legend AURELIA, and beneath it, in tiny capitals, the depth — 412 FT.

Ennis kept his eyes on the laminate. Beckwith kept his on the legend.

"Sonar, Bridge."

"Bridge, Sonar."

"Cole — give me bearings on every hard return you have. Not the soft ones. The hard."

"Aye, sir." A pause; the small dry sound of Cole pressing one headphone to his ear hard enough to leave a mark in the foam. "Bridge, Sonar — hard return, bearing two-five-eight, range nine hundred. Hard return, bearing two-six-zero, range one thousand one hundred. Soft returns — broadband — bearing two-six-one through two-six-four, multiple. Hard return, bearing two-six-two, range one thousand four hundred, and — stand by — bearing drift on that last, sir. Down-slope drift."

"Time them. I want a separation between hard and soft."

"Aye, sir."

Ennis plotted each return as Cole called it. The hard returns sat where the cone widened; the soft returns spread across it; the down-slope drift bent each hard return a degree, then a half-degree more, along the line the current set had taken. It was no clean fan. It was a man's pencil approximating the slow falling apart of a submarine into a shelf of water that had its own opinions about where things ought to go — and where it widened most, it took in the square that said AURELIA.

Beckwith took one slow breath. The drawer in his stateroom and the envelope inside it sat at the back of his mind like a weight he had set down somewhere and would have to come back for. He set the grease pencil down beside the plot.

"Number One."

"Sir."

"Make a note. Twenty-three fifty-three Zulu, our intercepted fragments, Sierra Three. Twenty-three fifty-five Zulu, sonar reports hard returns drifting two-five-eight through two-six-two, range building. Drift southwest into the Aurelia standoff envelope, projected."

"Projected, aye."

"Make a separate note. Aurelia is a commercial installation."

Ennis wrote it down. The pencil sat in his hand a beat after the words; he set it on top of the page rather than uncap it again.

"Sir."

"Number One."

"That's an inhabited structure."

"It is."

The wardroom intercom did not click. Nothing on the boat clicked. The pumps held. The bulkhead held. The boat held. The breakup outside her hull went on falling toward a chart square at the bottom of the slope in a current Beckwith had not designed for and could not, standing here three hundred feet up on the shelf with his palm marked in red ink, command.

He took up the handset.

"Sonar, Bridge."

"Bridge, Sonar."

"Cole. Read back the bearings on the hard returns from twenty-three fifty-one forward."

"Bridge, Sonar — bearing two-five-eight, range nine-fifty, drift southwest. Bearing two-six-zero, range one thousand two hundred, drift southwest. Bearing two-six-two, range one thousand five hundred, drift southwest. Bearing two-six-three —"

"Hold."

"Holding, sir."

"Cole. The square on the chart at bearing two-five-eight, range fourteen hundred. The one with the standoff ring. Read it to me."

The handset was silent for a moment. Beckwith could hear, faintly, Cole's chair scrape; could hear, more faintly, the sound of a young man leaning to the chart he had been issued and reading what the chart told him to read. The collar of Cole's dungaree shirt sat too high on his neck, the way it sat on a sailor whose neck had not yet caught up with his shirt; his face under the red lamps had a sheen.

"Bridge, Sonar." Cole's voice was as flat as he could make it, and his circuit discipline was as good as the manual asked. "The square at bearing two-five-eight, range fourteen hundred, is Aurelia, sir. Commercial undersea installation. Depth four-one-two. Operations begin this date."

"Acknowledged."

The handset would have permitted Cole to leave it there, and the manual would have approved. He stayed on the circuit. There was a half breath, and then the boy on the other end of it added the word a boy adds when he has done the math the manual did not ask him to do.

"Civilians, sir."

Beckwith held the handset against the side of his face and let the red light hold the room.

"Cole."

"Sir."

"Say that again."
